title: Dating
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Dating
summary: While Scripture does not address modern dating directly, it provides principles of wisdom, purity, intentionality, and mutual honor that shape how believers approach romantic relationships.
body:
The Bible does not contain a chapter on dating — the concept did not exist in the ancient world. But it provides principles that translate directly into how believers pursue romantic relationships today.

The first principle is intentionality. Proverbs warns against being led by impulse, urging instead that the heart be guarded carefully because it shapes the direction of life [verse:prov.4.23]. Romantic relationships are not exempt from this counsel. Paul adds a second principle: do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers, a metaphor drawn from agriculture that pictures two animals pulling in different directions [verse:2cor.6.14].

Honor is central. Paul instructs the Thessalonians that each person should learn to control their own body in holiness and honor, not in the passion of lust [verse:1thess.4.3-5]. This is not anti-desire — the Song of Solomon celebrates desire — but it insists that desire operate within a framework of respect and restraint.

Ruth and Boaz model a relationship built on character recognition. Boaz noticed Ruth's integrity before her appearance, and Ruth valued Boaz's kindness before his wealth [verse:ruth.3.10-11]. Their relationship progressed through public, accountable steps rather than hidden impulse.

Wisdom means seeking counsel. Plans fail without advice, but with many advisors they succeed [verse:prov.15.22]. Dating decisions made in isolation tend toward self-deception; community provides perspective.

verses.annotation: Guard your heart — it determines the course of your life, including romantic decisions.
Paul's unequal yoke warning — shared spiritual direction matters in partnership.
Holiness and honor in how we treat our own bodies and those of others.
Boaz recognizes Ruth's character — a relationship built on integrity, not infatuation.
Plans succeed with many advisors — community counsel in romantic decisions.
Do not awaken love until it pleases — a caution against forcing or rushing romance.

scholars.quote: Longman interprets Song of Solomon 2:7 as a wisdom warning against premature romantic entanglement — love has its own timing and should not be artificially accelerated.
Fee explains that Paul's 'unequal yoke' in 2 Corinthians 6:14 draws on Deuteronomy's prohibition of mismatched plow animals — the point is functional incompatibility, not superiority.
scholars.source: The Second Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, NICNT, 2014)

scholars.quote: Hubbard observes that Ruth and Boaz's relationship followed publicly visible, community-accountable steps — each advance was witnessed, each commitment was formal, modeling a relationship built on transparency.
